revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:土葬]]
'''Burial''' (土葬, dosō) is the practice of interring the deceased directly into the ground.
== Overview == Burial is a form of interment where the body is placed underground. There are two main methods: directly placing the body in the ground or enclosing it in a coffin before burial.
The oldest known burials date back to the Neanderthals, making burial one of the oldest forms of interment.
Many religions, such as {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|qid=Q5043|lt=Christianity|1=Christianity}}, {{ill|Islam|en|Islam|qid=Q432|lt=Islam|1=Islam}}, and {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}}, oppose {{ill|Cremation|en|Cremation|qid=Q207315|lt=Cremation|1=Cremation}} and favor burial. According to {{ill|BBC|en|BBC|qid=Q9531|lt=BBC|1=BBC}}, burial is permitted in over 190 countries worldwide.[https://mainichi.jp/articles/20210227/ddm/007/040/069000c "Sri Lanka Restores Burial Amid Protests Against Forced Cremations"] 『{{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|en|Mainichi Shimbun|qid=Q1136866|lt=Mainichi Shimbun|1=Mainichi Shimbun}}』, February 27, 2021 (International Section), accessed March 3, 2021
== Burial in Japan == [[File:Temporary cemetery in Ishinomaki.JPEG|thumb|Temporary burial following the [[:en:Great East Japan Earthquake|Great East Japan Earthquake]] ([[Ishinomaki, Miyagi Prefecture|Ishinomaki]], {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|qid=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}, June 7, 2011)]] The "[[Daika Reform]]" of 646, recorded in the 『[[Nihon Shoki]]』, includes burial regulations based on the [[:en:Confucian|Confucian]] principles in the 『[[:en:Lǐjì|Lǐjì]]』 ("Funeral Rites" section) and the 『{{ill|Records of the Three Kingdoms|en|Records of the Three Kingdoms|qid=Q247318|lt=Sanguozhi|1=Records of the Three Kingdoms}}』’s account of the {{ill|Wei|en|Wei|qid=Q374815|lt=Wei|1=Wei}} emperor {{ill|Cao Pi|en|Cao Pi|qid=Q313333|lt=Cao Pi|1=Cao Pi}} (Yellow Beginning 3, 222 CE).
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the spread of Confucianism influenced anti-Buddhist policies. Figures such as {{ill|Hoshina Masayuki|en|Hoshina Masayuki|qid=Q837236|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|1=Hoshina Masayuki}} of the {{ill|Aizu Domain|en|Aizu Domain|qid=Q4389944|lt=Aizu Domain|1=Aizu Domain}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|en|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|qid=Q1194944|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} and [[Tokugawa Nariaki]] of the {{ill|Mito Domain|en|Mito Domain|qid=Q1146904|lt=Mito Domain|1=Mito Domain}}, and [[Nonaka Kenzan]] of the {{ill|Tosa Domain|en|Tosa Domain|qid=Q1189704|lt=Tosa Domain|1=Tosa Domain}} promoted Confucian-style burials and banned cremation in favor of burial.Nishino Koichi, "[https://doi.org/10.5845/bukkyobunka.1999.92 On the Perception of Cremation in the Prohibition of Cremation Decree of Meiji 6]" 『Buddhist Culture Bulletin』, Vol. 8 (1999), pp. 92-115, {{doi|10.5845/bukkyobunka.1999.92}} However, Tosa later differentiated burials for criminals (cremated) and civilians (buried)."Fuzoku Jibutsu Compilation," March 15, 1995, Kochi Public Library, p. 19.
The {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|qid=Q1322181|lt=Government of Meiji Japan|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} initially opposed cremation due to its Buddhist associations and issued a ban on July 18, 1873. Large-scale burial grounds, such as {{ill|Aoyama Cemetery|en|Aoyama Cemetery|qid=Q1036028|lt=Aoyama Cemetery|1=Aoyama Cemetery}}, were established in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} for this purpose. Despite this, opposition from Buddhist factions and public health concerns led to the repeal of the cremation ban in 1875. Modern crematorium standards were introduced, and cremation eventually became dominant, surpassing 50% of all interments by 1935.Ukai Hidetoku, "[https://business.nikkei.com/atcl/book/16/102400002/102800004/ 'Society Without Burial']," Business Nikkei.
Burial customs in Japan vary by region. Coffins may be round or rectangular, and there are traditions such as scattering coins or protecting graves from wildlife with bamboo. Bodies might be exhumed after several years to retrieve bones for reinterment in urns. Although the "[[Cemetery, Burial, and Funeral Act]]" recognizes both cremation and burial equally, urban areas like {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} and {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} often prohibit burial due to space constraints."[https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASSDN4K7DSDNUNHB00LM.html Miyagi Prefecture to Consider Burial Cemeteries for Indonesian Laborers]," Asahi Shimbun Digital, December 21, 2024. Burials persist in certain rural regions such as {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, [[Nara]], and {{ill|Wakayama|en|Wakayama|qid=Q16909571|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama}} Prefectures and in isolated areas.
== Burial in China == In {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, Confucian values historically favored burial, and it remained dominant until {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} introduced cremation during the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}. By the [[Qianlong]] era of the {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|qid=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}}, cremation had significantly declined. In modern [[:en:People's Republic of China|China]], cremation is mandatory in most areas due to land and public health considerations, except for specific groups such as {{ill|Uyghurs|en|Uyghurs|qid=Q46118|lt=Uyghurs|1=Uyghurs}} and {{ill|Kazakhs|en|Kazakhs|qid=Q80040|lt=Kazakhs|1=Kazakhs}}.
== Burial in Korea == {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} traditionally practiced Confucian-style burials. Though burial was once widespread, cremation rates have increased to over 85% due to land constraints and shifting lifestyles.[https://japan-korea-strait8.org/colum/20161201001.html "Traditional Korean Funerary Customs"], Japan-Korea Strait Governors Conference.
== Christian Burial == In the {{ill|United States|en|United States|qid=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}}, burial was historically preferred due to Christian beliefs in bodily resurrection. However, economic factors have increased the prevalence of cremation, with 45.3% of Americans choosing cremation by 2013.{{Cite web|url=http://www.cnn.co.jp/business/CNN200904150027.html|title="Changing Funeral Trends in the U.S.," CNN, April 15, 2009.}}
== Islamic Burial == {{ill|Islam|en|Islam|qid=Q432|lt=Islam|1=Islam}} mandates burial whenever possible, emphasizing the dignity of the deceased. Bodies are washed, wrapped in cloth, and buried without a coffin in a north-facing direction toward {{ill|Mecca|en|Mecca|qid=Q5806|lt=Mecca|1=Mecca}}. In Japan, Muslim burials are permitted in select cemeteries.[https://web.archive.org/web/20180826224745/http://wasegg.com/archives/1013 "Muslim Burial Practices in Japan," Waseda University, March 21, 2018.]
== Footnotes == {{Reflist|2}}
